chunks.title: Introduction to Animal-Based Diet
chunks.summary: The speaker emphasizes the significance of diet in influencing health and illness. An overview of the podcast's focus on an animal-based diet is provided, including steps for implementation and a discussion on new research regarding sugars and sweeteners.
chunks.transcript: and I believe that diet is the single greatest lever that we can use to affect health or illness in our bodies in the bodies of our families our kids our brothers our sisters our parents everyone on the earth on this week's episode of the podcast I wanted to start the year off high level what is an animal-based diet how do you do this what is animal-based Diet 101 in this podcast I'm going to walk you through this step by step I'm going to give you four steps for things to remove from your diet and four steps for things to add to your diet to really make your diet an animal-based diet which I believe is the optimal starting point for humans if you've listened to my podcast before a lot of the concepts in this one are going to be familiar but I always try to put in new pieces of information and this one specifically I talk about high fructose corn syrup differences in sugars and artificial sweeteners something I haven't talked about on the podcast in the past but some new data that I came across is very compelling so I think you guys will enjoy this one hope your year is starting off well here's to an amazing healthy and fruitful and vital year

chunks.title: Dr. Perspective on Diet and Health
chunks.summary: The speaker shares their background as a doctor, stressing the lack of emphasis on diet in medical education and its crucial role in addressing chronic illnesses. They argue for a shift towards nutritional education in healthcare settings.
chunks.transcript: believe that diet is the single greatest lever that we can use to affect health or illness in our bodies in the bodies of our families our kids our brothers our sisters our parents everyone on the Earth a very very small percentage less than one percent probably less than a tenth of a percent of the illness that we observe within Western medicine is related to immutable that is unchangeable genetic defects quote unquote um these are genetic quote mutations that are unfixable the majority of what we see 99.5 99.7 perhaps even 99.9 percent of what we see in Western medicine is chronic illness that I think is ameliorated improved sometimes completely reversed with dietary changes so why within Western medicine is the majority of what we learn Pharmaceuticals which don't address the root cause and we really are never challenged as physicians in medical school and residency or Healthcare practitioners nurse practitioners Pas even nurses to think about the root cause of illness if so much of what we treat as doctors and nurses and nurse practitioners and PAs is malleable with diet at least half of what we learn in medical schools should be about nutrients and dietary strategies and the literature regarding various diets and the literature of suggesting mechanisms and having debates about seed oils pro and con and having debates about artificial sweeteners this is what should be happening in medical school sadly none of that happens which is why I do what I do and why many others who are also doing great work do what they do because we're trying to educate so I no longer see patients I do educational stuff because I've realized that I can affect more lives positively in this way and this is what I feel called to this is what I enjoy the most I found medical

chunks.title: Defining an Optimal Starting Point for Human Diet
chunks.summary: The discussion moves toward defining an optimal starting point for human diets, emphasizing the need for nutrient-dense, bioavailable foods with minimal toxins. The speaker shares their belief that there is no single optimal diet, but highlights a foundational diet based on animal products.
chunks.transcript: exploration I am looking at all of this through a lens of trying to find a diet for humans that has the most nutrients the most bioavailability of those nutrients meaning if the nutrients the vitamins the minerals the cofactors the peptides are present in food that we can absorb them right so not just the most absolute amount of nutrients the most bioavailable amount of nutrients that adds a whole other layer I'm looking for a diet that has the least amount of toxins and by toxins I mean things like heavy metals para floral alkylated substances also known as pfas BPA BPS xenoestrogens microplastics but also plant defense chemicals which I would see as toxic for many individuals the degree of that toxicity will vary from person to person mileage may vary but I do believe that many plant chemicals are harmful for us and a lot of people do better when they eliminate these so that's the framework that I'm using to think about diet what has the most nutrients what aligns best with our human metabolism as well what allows our mitochondria to function well in the best way possible based on history and evolution anthropology and biochemistry and medicine and what has the least amount of toxins toxins that can harm us toxins that can inhibit the absorption nutrients that's the framework that's the question I'm trying to answer because that's how I would Define broadly what an optimal diet dietary starting point for humans would be and from the outset I will say that my bias is that our history tells us a lot of these answers I don't believe we are smart enough to design Soylent Green which for those of you who aren't familiar is a I think historical literature reference that has actually been made into a real drink just a liquid drink that has everything humans need and you can just drink it and it's super easy never have to think about food I don't think most of us would want that type of experience with food some of us might for convenience but I don't think that exists because I think that there are even nutrients vitamins minerals cofactors that we're not aware of in the foods that we eat and by mimicking what humans have done for hundreds of thousands of years as Homo sapiens or millions of years as pre-homosapien hominids Homo habilism erectus I think that we fill in a lot of that nutritional dark matter just like within your gut and my gut 60 plus percent of the microbes viruses fungi are uncharacterized we don't even know what they are I think a lot of nutrition hopefully not

chunks.title: Four Steps to Remove from Your Diet
chunks.summary: The speaker introduces the four essential removal steps for creating an animal-based diet: eliminating seed oils, high fructose corn syrup, artificial sweeteners, and vegetables. Each removal step is discussed with reasons and health implications.
chunks.transcript: step-by-step guide here of how to make your diet the optimal starting point for humans so four removal steps and then four addition steps and I think if you get through those eight steps which can be done for removal steps first and then the four addition steps where you can do them in parallel add something take something out add something take something out but I wanted to make it step by step so that you guys can understand what I think the most important steps are the whole podcast is framed from the perspective of an animal-based diet something that I believe is the optimal starting point for humans and I'll tell you what that is and then I will reverse engineer it in some ways and talk about the steps that I've used to get to that point in the podcast so an animal-based diet is organs and meat the most nutrient-rich foods for humans that I've ever seen prized by hunter-gatherers throughout Revolution clearly prized today organs and meat for protein and nutrients and some animal fats Dairy preferably raw dairy as an animal food that is nutritious for humans and a source of calcium and other unique nutrients eggs animal Foods so the animal foods are there and then a carbohydrate source for humans from fruit and honey which I believe to be the least toxic sources of carbohydrates so an animal-based diet is succinctly put organs meat fruit honey raw dairy it's kind of just a term that I've coined again I want you to question these things and think for yourself and not just accept this as Canon but that's what I mean when I say an animal based that can harm us like gluten or other lectins you end up with something akin to an animal-based diet I'll talk about proportions and macros in this podcast as well to help you guys break it down and if you did nothing else but remove these things from your diet these four steps that I'm about to give you I think your diet would improve and you would feel better you don't even have to add anything I think that if you just cut these Foods out you will become healthier obviously the optimal condition for this pursuit of the optimal human diet the optimal condition for this adventure that you're going to go on maybe your family will go on is to both add things and take things away so add the good things take the negative things away first step step one in terms of the form removal steps if chunks.title: The Health Impacts of Seed Oils
chunks.summary: The speaker explains the negative health impacts of seed oils and the significance of reducing their consumption for overall health improvement. Details about types of seed oils and their detrimental effects are provided.

removing seed oils will mean that a lot of processed foods that you may have enjoyed previously will not be on the menu but again removing seed oils doing that one thing I believe will result in chunks.title: High Fructose Corn Syrup Concerns
chunks.summary: The focus shifts to high fructose corn syrup, highlighting its unique properties and potential for weight gain compared to regular sugar. The speaker urges listeners to cut it out of their diets, recommending natural sweeteners instead.
chunks.transcript: remove high fructose corn syrup from your diet this one may be more familiar to most of you but there's some Nuance here that's important to point out high fructose corn syrup is different than sucrose it's a completely different product made from a corn syrup that appears to be more fattening more weight gain inducing in animal models and probably in humans but certainly in animal models even relative to sucrose I'm not a huge fan of table sugar I don't know why you would eat table sugar when there are better ways to get carbohydrates in a simple form that's going to be easily digestible like fruit good freshly squeezed or made fruit juice and honey or maple syrup but at a biochemical level pure sucrose which is a disaccharide of glucose and fructose is different than high fructose corn syrup now in terms of the fructose concentrations fructose in sucrose will be 50 50 with glucose and high fructose corn syrup it's perhaps a little higher 55 45 fructose to glucose but that five percent of fructose is probably not the real issue here it appears to be corn starch hidden starches or hidden sources of calories in the high fructose corn syrup that make it particularly fattening quote unquote particularly weight gain inducing so we've been told for many generations that sucrose quote-unquote sugar is bad for us and I think that the more accurate thing I would say is high fructose corn syrup is bad for you and it's really bad for you so don't give your kids high fructose corn syrup I actually wouldn't have a problem recommending honey maple syrup or fruit fruit juice even to my niece and nephew and I've had those conversations with my sister and in my own life in the clients that I've worked with fruit honey maple syrup fruit juice even do not raise hemoglobin A1c significantly they do not raise fasting glucose they do not raise fasting insulin they don't make you more insulin resistant I've spoken about that at length in the past so number two remove high fructose corn syrup I'm going to do a whole podcast on different types of sugars and artificial sweeteners probably next week so stay tuned for that but I did want to share this article if you're watching on YouTube high fructose corn syrup prompts considerably more weight gain researchers find this is March 22 from Princeton University I'm looking at a Science Daily report so it's kind of high level the summary as researchers demonstrate that all sweeteners are not equal when it comes to weight gain rats with access to high fructose corn syrup gain significantly more weight than those with access to table sugar even when their overall caloric intake was the same that pretty much says it all um and as I said I would say that and this will trigger some keto Advocates that sucrose may not be harmful for humans at least literature begins to ask us to make that question in our lives but why would you do that I don't eat sucrose or table sugar in my life I don't see a point to that if you look at my previous podcast from last year in 2022 with chronometer you'll see that maple syrup has manganese and coconut water has magnesium and honey has nitric oxide precursors and we know that maple syrup has other nutrients and minerals um we know that we also know that molasses or other less processed forms of sugar have nutrients and minerals so I would rather get manganese and magnesium and other micronutrients in the foods that I'm eating then eat pure sugar for my calories but it begs an interesting question regarding the biochemistry of all this and high

chunks.title: Artificial Sweeteners and Their Dangers
chunks.summary: The discussion covers potential dangers of artificial sweeteners, citing recent studies that show how they can disrupt gut microbiota and insulin sensitivity. The speaker encourages listeners to remove these from their diets as well.
chunks.transcript: this one may surprise you guys so this is important not to ignore or Miss remove artificial sweeteners there's been a lot of back and forth in the nutrition sphere about artificial sweeteners yes they don't have calories but there's some very interesting research both on the way that artificial sweeteners things like Stevia sucralose which is Splenda aspartame can interrupt Quorum sensing which is communication between the microbes and your guts and even more compelling to me is the research from Dana small at Yale University hopefully I'll get her on the podcast soon but I'm going to show an article from her group from 2020 and what happened in this article was that Splenda was added to a drink with dextrose so sucralose a non-coloric sweetener was added to a drink with dextrose which is an oligosaccharide of glucose so pure glucose so there's no fructose in this drink and what you have then when humans consume this drink you have a mismatch between presented nutrients and sweetness and what happened in both adults humans and especially in adolescent humans was that when carbohydrates were presented with artificial sweeteners specifically sucralose in this case people became insulin resistant really fast there were metabolic derangements I've never heard anyone talk about this in the health space and hats up to my friend Andrew huberman who turned me on to this research but Andrew hasn't spoken about it yet on his podcast I'm sure he will but so many of the advocates for artificial sweeteners I've never heard them address this I don't think they're aware of this now to be complete in my recount of this study's findings and I'll show you the reference in a moment if you're watching on YouTube when pure sucralose was given in a drink there was no negative effect but how often do humans only drink something with sucralose or only eat something with sucralose that has no other carbohydrates naturally occurring or how often do we eat something with sucralose or drink something with sucralose with other Foods so there is a possibility you would not escape the way that sucralose and other artificial sweeteners will affect the communication between your gut microbes but there's a possibility that you could have a Diet Coke in the middle of the day with nothing else no calories no carbohydrates and that may not be as harmful to your metabolism as drinking a diet coke with a I don't know whatever you want to eat a piece of toast a piece of fruit a hamburger anything with carbohydrates in it with that sucralose containing beverage or food is going to mess up your body's connections between sweetness and present calories and that leads to insulin resistance in humans it also does it in animal studies but this is in humans guys so the other thing to note is that there are over 3 000 Foods probably close to 4 000 Foods at this point that have sucralose in them and added calories in the form of sugar why would they do that well sucralose is sweet so sucralose is also cheaper than sugar so manufacturers can make something sweeter and say that it has less sugar and they can cut costs but you're absolutely messing up the metabolism I'm thinking about a lot of drinks I believe Logan Paul's drink Prime has an artificial sweetener and sugar in it and a lot of these drinks are doing this now saying they have less sugar but they're giving you sugar they're giving you carbohydrates and they're giving you an artificial sweetener that is very bad and in fact I think they would be better off from a metabolic perspective either doing pure sucralose which I don't think is the right option or pure sugar not high fructose corn syrup from a metabolic perspective pure sugar in those drinks would be better than sucralose plus sugar so these drinks are a metabolic nightmare for humans and yet we've just begun to understand this in the research and no one is being held accountable for this and they're being marketed as lower sugar this is actually a really really important finding something that you should not ignore so let me show you this article title of the paper is short-term consumption of sucralose with but not without carbohydrate impairs neural and metabolic sensitivity to sugar in humans it's from cell metabolism 2020 again the final author is Dana small you can find her YouTube presentation at the neuron conference which talks about this at around minute 36. so I gave you guys a summary of what happened in this if you really want to go read the paper you can see it but this is a metabolic nightmare for humans and no one should ignore this and no one who is saying that artificial sweeteners are benign should be allowed to say that without explaining this finding and explaining how this is somehow benign for humans I think this is horrible for humans so step three remove artificial sweeteners do not mess with this stuff guys and yes this means

chunks.title: Controversial Removal of Vegetables
chunks.summary: The speaker controversially advocates for the removal of vegetables from the diet, providing reasons based on their beliefs about the negative effects of certain plant components. This segment addresses common misconceptions about vegetable health benefits.
chunks.transcript: remove vegetables this is obviously the most controversial one but I think it's important now let me describe what vegetables are these are the non-fruit parts of plants this is technical definition avocado is a fruit guys avocados not a vegetable squash is a fruit not a vegetable okay leaves and stems and seeds of plants are vegetables roots are technically vegetables as well removing leaves and stems and seeds from your diet I believe will improve your health specially seeds now seeds are seeds nuts grains and beans I'm not going to go into all that in this podcast but suffice it to say that beans and grains are problematic for humans from a digestive enzyme perspective containing digestive enzyme Inhibitors containing phytic acid containing lectins allow gluten are messing with your digestion I think grains and beans are definitely messing with your digestion I think that leaves are highly defended and have problematic compounds in them that are not good for steps one two and three will remove seed oils

chunks.title: Four Steps to Add to Your Diet
chunks.summary: After outlining the removal steps, the speaker introduces the four addition steps for enhancing an animal-based diet, focusing on the inclusion of nutrient-dense organs, high-quality meat, and safe carbohydrates. The emphasis is on a holistic approach to diet improvement.

chunks.title: Importance of Organs in Nutrition
chunks.summary: Highlighting the nutritional value of organ meats, particularly liver and heart, the speaker encourages listeners to incorporate these into their diets for optimal health. The unique nutrients and health benefits associated with organ consumption are discussed.

chunks.title: Meat and Macronutrient Guidance
chunks.summary: The speaker provides guidelines on meat consumption and macronutrient distribution, discussing the balance of protein, carbohydrates, and fats necessary for a well-rounded animal-based diet. Personal anecdotes and science-backed rationale are included.

chunks.title: Final Thoughts on an Animal-Based Diet
chunks.summary: In the closing segment, the speaker reiterates their view on the principles of an animal-based diet, encouraging experimentation and personal adaptation. They emphasize a community approach and continued exploration of dietary strategies.
